raw_case:
Exploring an Uncommon Presentation of Kikuchi-Fujimoto Disease: Case Insights
Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease (KFD) is a self-limiting, benign illness that is typified by cervical lymphadenopathy, typically accompanied by a low fever and night sweats. Loss of weight, nausea, vomiting, and sore throat are fewer common symptoms. KFD can have an acute or subacute start, and it usually develops over two to three weeks. Although viral aetiology is still a concept that needs further research, the clinical, histological, and immunohistochemical aspects seem to support it. Since specific diagnostic laboratory tests are not available, the diagnosis is frequently established by excising a sample of the affected lymph nodes. This case study features a 63-year-old male patient who first complained of fever and dyspnoea. Upon further investigation, the patient's condition was determined to be Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease, which was treated appropriately. 

Introduction

Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease (KFD) is an uncommon inflammatory illness that primarily affects younger people and paediatric patients. The typical presentation, which can range from acute to subacute, is marked by painful, tender lymphadenopathy together with systemic symptoms such as fever, malaise, weight loss, arthralgias, and other cutaneous indications [1].There is much speculation about the aetiology of KFD. A viral or autoimmune cause has been suggested. The role of Epstein-Barr virus as well as other viruses (human herpesvirus 6 (HHV6), HHV8, parvovirus B19) in the pathogenesis of KFD remains controversial and not convincingly demonstrated. Despite this, Hudnall et al. have reported that clinical manifestations such as upper respiratory prodrome, atypical lymphocytosis, and specific histopathological characteristics may indicate the possibility of a viral infection [2].KFD has additionally been observed in individuals who are HIV- and human T-lymphotropic virus (HTLV-1)-positive. However, tubular reticular structures have been found in the cytoplasm of activated lymphocytes and histiocytes in individuals with KFD, according to electron microscopic investigations. A conclusive diagnosis requires an excisional lymph node biopsy, which reveals a neutrophil and eosinophil deficit. Histiocytes positive for myeloperoxidase and cluster of differentiation 68 (CD68) and a negligible amount of B cells will all be seen by immunohistochemistry. Because fine-needle aspiration obtains a limited amount of tissue, it is usually insufficient for the confirmation of the diagnosis. It is crucial to distinguish KFD from lymphomas and infectious aetiologies. Cultures and serological testing can provide more evidence in support of the histologic diagnosis [3].

Case presentation

A 63-year-old male patient presented to the respiratory medicine department with the chief complaints of fever on and off for 15 days and dry cough associated with dyspnea on exertion for seven days. The patient also had a history of loss of appetite and loss of weight of 5-6 kg in two months. The patient had a known case of bilateral recurrent pleural effusion. There was no history of associated chest pain, hemoptysis, pleural tuberculosis or any comorbidities. A general examination revealed pallor and clubbing. On the examination of vitals, tachypnea and hypoxia were noted. A respiratory system examination revealed bilaterally reduced breathing sounds. The findings of lab investigations are given in Table 1. Chest X-ray (Figure 1) was done, which was suggestive of bilateral pleural effusion on the right side more than the left for which therapeutic tapping was done, and 1500 ml fluid was drained (Figures 2, 3). Lymph node examination was done, which was suggestive of multiple palpable lymph nodes predominantly (left posterior auricular, left posterior cervical and right occipital group of lymph nodes). Acid-fast bacillus (AFB) test and Trunet assay of the sputum and pleural fluid were found to be negative. Pleural fluid investigations suggested exudative pleural effusion with low adenosine deaminase (ADA) according to light criteria. Biopsy showed lymph nodes with follicular hyperplasia (lymphoid) and focal, circumscribed, paracortical necrotizing lesions composed of abundant karyorrhectic debris, and a collection of large mononuclear cell bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) (culture and sensitivity) showed no growth. A high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the thorax was suggestive of bilateral pleural effusion with sub-segmental atelectasis with bilateral patchy ground-glass opacifications (GGOs) and consolidation (Figure 4).Duplex colour doppler of the bilateral lower limb (arterial and venous) was found to be normal and no deep vein thrombosis was found.Ultrasonography (USG)-guided cervical lymph node fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) was done and the histopathology reports were suggestive of reactive lymphoid hyperplasia with necrotic changes (Figures 5, 6), which was diagnosed to be Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease for which the patient was started on corticosteroids. The patient was discharged when symptomatically better.

Discussion

KFD is an incredibly unusual condition with an acute or subacute start that develops over two to three weeks. It is nearly always accompanied by cervical lymphadenopathy, which consists of sore lymph nodes primarily involving the posterior cervical triangle. Usually, KFD resolves on its own in three to four months. Recurrence rates of 3-4% have been documented, which is low but conceivable [4].In a small percentage of people, systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) may develop years later. There appears to be little danger to family members of the patients with KFD with regard to the disease. It is important to use symptomatic treatments to relieve the upsetting local and systemic complaints. The use of analgesics, antipyretics, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) can help reduce fever and soreness in the lymph nodes. There are no established diagnostic standards for KFD because of its uncommon nature and inconsistent presentation. Clinicians must first rule out other possible causes of lymphadenopathy, such as infectious, neoplastic, and autoimmune origins, in order to make a diagnosis. Although its effectiveness is questionable, the administration of corticosteroids has been advised in cases of severe extra-nodal or widespread KFD. To perform a diagnostic excisional lymph node biopsy, surgical consultation may be necessary. To rule out the development of SLE, patients with KFD need to undergo a comprehensive survey and ongoing follow-up for a few years. After a definitive diagnosis, the cervical lymphadenopathy has a benign course and seems to go away on its own within one to six months [5,6].

Conclusions

This case report highlights a rare presentation of Kikuchi-Fujimoto Disease (KFD) in a 63-year-old male, characterized by bilateral recurrent pleural effusions and significant respiratory symptoms. The final diagnosis of KFD was established through ultrasound-guided FNAC of the cervical lymph nodes, revealing reactive lymphoid hyperplasia with necrotic changes. Treatment for KFD generally involves supportive care, with NSAIDs and glucocorticoids providing significant symptomatic relief. This case emphasizes the importance of including KFD in the differential diagnosis for patients presenting with lymphadenopathy, even in the presence of symptoms like pleural effusion. The histopathological evaluation was crucial in reaching an accurate diagnosis and directing appropriate treatment strategies. The patient's favourable response to corticosteroid therapy and subsequent discharge in a stable condition emphasizes the potential for positive outcomes with appropriate recognition and management of this rare disorder. Understanding the clinical and histopathological features of KFD and its treatment options can help clinicians provide optimal care and avoid unnecessary procedures.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 63-year-old male patient.
- **Chief Complaint:** Fever on and off for 15 days, dry cough with dyspnea on exertion for seven days.
- **History of Present Illness:** Loss of appetite and weight (5-6 kg over two months); recurrent bilateral pleural effusion. No history of chest pain, hemoptysis, tuberculosis, or any comorbid conditions.
- **Past Medical History:** Bilateral recurrent pleural effusion.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Pallor, clubbing, tachypnea, hypoxia; reduced bilateral breathing sounds on respiratory examination.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Chest X-ray: Bilateral pleural effusion (right > left). 
  - Acid-fast bacillus test and Trunet assay (sputum, pleural fluid): Negative.
  - Pleural fluid analysis: Exudative effusion with low adenosine deaminase (ADA). 
  - Biopsy: Lymph nodes with follicular hyperplasia (lymphoid) and focal paracortical necrotizing lesions with karyorrhectic debris.
  - High-resolution Chest CT (HRCT): Bilateral pleural effusion, sub-segmental atelectasis, bilateral patchy ground-glass opacifications (GGOs), and consolidation.
  - Duplex Color Doppler (lower limbs): Normal; no deep vein thrombosis.
  - USG-guided cervical lymph node FNAC: Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia with necrotic changes.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Workup:**
   - The patient's signs and symptoms (prolonged fever, dyspnea, pleural effusion, lymphadenopathy, and weight loss) prompted consideration of infectious, neoplastic, or autoimmune conditions.
  
2. **Infectious Causes:** 
   - Tuberculosis was initially considered but ruled out based on negative acid-fast bacillus tests, Trunet assay, and exudative pleural effusion with low ADA levels.
   - Bacterial infections were less likely based on the absence of pleural fluid culture growth.
  
3. **Neoplastic Causes:**
   - Lymphoma was considered due to significant lymphadenopathy, but cervical lymph node FNAC reported reactive hyperplasia, excluding malignancy.
  
4. **Autoimmune or Rare Causes:**
   - Kikuchi-Fujimoto Disease (KFD) was suspected due to the histopathological findings (necrotic lesions with karyorrhectic debris, reactive lymphoid hyperplasia).
   - Systemic lupus erythematosus was ruled out based on lack of clinical or serological evidence.
  
5. **Confirmatory Tests:**
   - FNAC findings supported KFD diagnosis. Negative infectious markers and biopsy findings helped exclude other causes.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The final diagnosis was **Kikuchi-Fujimoto Disease (KFD)** based on clinical presentation (fever, lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion) and confirmatory FNAC findings, which showed reactive lymphoid hyperplasia with necrotic changes characteristic of KFD. The absence of evidence for infectious, neoplastic, or autoimmune conditions further supported the conclusion. The patient responded favorably to corticosteroid therapy, consistent with the management of KFD.

---

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case step by step. The patient is a 63-year-old male with a fever on and off for 15 days, dry cough, and dyspnea on exertion for seven days. He's also had weight loss and loss of appetite over two months. Recurrent bilateral pleural effusion is a key part of his history. 

First, I need to consider infections. The AFB test and Trunet assay (assuming this is for tuberculosis) are negative, so TB seems less likely. But other infections like bacterial or viral could still be possible. However, the exudative effusion with low ADA makes TB less probable because ADA is often elevated in TB pleuritis. The pleural fluid analysis here points away from TB.

Next, looking at the pleural effusion: exudative. Common causes include malignancy, infections, autoimmune diseases. Since infections are less likely here (negative tests), I need to think about malignancy. His age and weight loss are red flags for cancer. The recurrent effusion could be due to metastatic disease, mesothelioma, or lymphoma. But the biopsy results of lymph nodes show follicular hyperplasia and necrotizing lesions. FNAC of cervical nodes shows reactive hyperplasia with necrosis. That's interesting. Reactive changes could be due to infection, but also some cancers like lymphoma might present with necrosis. Wait, follicular hyperplasia is more benign, but the necrotizing lesions might suggest something else.

The HRCT shows ground-glass opacities and consolidation. These findings can be seen in interstitial lung diseases, pneumonia, or maybe even organizing pneumonia. But combined with pleural effusion, the differentials shift. Autoimmune causes like lupus or rheumatoid arthritis can cause pleuritis and effusions. However, the patient has no mentioned joint symptoms or other systemic signs. Also, the necrotizing lesions in lymph nodes might be a clue. Sarcoidosis can cause lymph node issues, but typically non-caseating granulomas, which aren't mentioned here. 

Biopsy shows lymphoid follicular hyperplasia and paracortical necrotizing lesions with karyorrhectic debris. Karyorrhexis refers to nuclear fragmentation. Necrotizing lymphadenitis can be seen in conditions like Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease, which is a self-limiting condition, but that's more common in younger people. However, other causes of necrotizing lymph nodes include systemic lupus erythematosus, tuberculosis (but AFB negative), or lymphoma. But the FNAC is reactive, not showing malignant cells. 

The combination of recurrent pleural effusions, weight loss, fever, and lymph node findings makes me think about lymphoma. But the biopsy shows follicular hyperplasia, which is reactive, not lymphoma. Wait, but maybe the biopsy is from the lymph node, and FNAC is also reactive. However, sometimes in lymphoma, especially Hodgkin's or certain non-Hodgkin types, there can be reactive changes alongside malignant cells. Maybe the biopsy isn't sufficient. Alternatively, could this be a paraneoplastic syndrome? 

Another angle: the presence of clubbing. Clubbing can be due to lung cancer, interstitial lung disease, or chronic infections. His clubbing, along with weight loss, makes me think of malignancy again. However, if the lymph nodes are reactive, maybe the primary is elsewhere. 

Looking at the HRCT findings: ground-glass opacities and consolidation. These could indicate interstitial lung disease, but with pleural effusion, maybe an underlying systemic disease. What about autoimmune conditions with pulmonary involvement, like rheumatoid arthritis or vasculitis? But again, lack of other symptoms. 

The pleural fluid is exudative. Let's recall Light's criteria. Exudative effusion suggests malignancy, infection, or autoimmune. Since TB is ruled out, and other infections are not confirmed, malignancy or autoimmune remain. The low ADA also supports non-TB. 

Another possibility is mesothelioma, given pleural effusions, but usually there's a history of asbestos exposure, which isn't mentioned here. 

The necrotizing lesions in lymph nodes could also be due to a granulomatous process, but the AFB is negative. Fungal infections? Maybe, but no tests mentioned. 

Wait, the patient has tachypnea and hypoxia. The GGOs and consolidation on CT could indicate some form of pneumonitis. Maybe an organizing pneumonia, which can be cryptogenic or secondary to connective tissue disease, infection, or drugs. But how does that tie in with the pleural effusions and lymphadenopathy? 

Putting it all together: recurrent bilateral exudative pleural effusions, weight loss, fever, lymphadenopathy with necrotizing changes but reactive on FNAC and biopsy. The presence of clubbing and hypoxia. The negative TB tests. The possible malignancy here is a strong contender. However, the lymph node biopsies are not showing malignancy. Could it be a disseminated cancer with reactive lymph nodes? Or perhaps a lymphoma that's not been sampled adequately. 

Alternatively, another systemic disease like Castleman disease, which can cause lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms. Or perhaps an autoimmune condition like IgG4-related disease, which can cause lymphadenopathy and pleural effusions. 

Wait, the necrotizing lymphadenitis with karyorrhectic debris is a key point. Kikuchi's disease is possible, but again, more common in young females. However, in older patients, it's less common. Another possibility is systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), as it can cause necrotizing lymphadenitis and pleuritis. But the patient is male, and there's no mention of other SLE symptoms like rash, arthritis, etc. 

Another angle: the pleural effusion and lymphadenopathy could be due to a malignancy like lymphoma or metastatic adenocarcinoma. However, the biopsies are showing reactive changes. Maybe the primary tumor is elsewhere, and the lymph nodes are reactive. For example, lung cancer with paraneoplastic syndrome. The HRCT shows GGOs and consolidation; could that be lymphangitic carcinomatosis? 

Alternatively, considering the patient's clubbing and weight loss, maybe a primary lung malignancy with pleural metastases. However, the CT doesn't mention any masses, but does have atelectasis and GGOs. Maybe a mesothelioma, but again, need exposure history. 

Wait, the patient has recurrent pleural effusion. If the cytology of the pleural fluid hasn't shown malignant cells, maybe it's a paramalignant effusion, where the effusion is due to the cancer but not directly invaded. 

Another possibility: tuberculosis is still possible despite negative AFB and ADA. Maybe the tests are false negatives. But the clinical picture isn't strongly supporting TB here. 

Wait, the pleural fluid ADA is low. ADA is elevated in TB pleuritis, so low ADA makes TB less likely. So probably not TB. 

What about fungal infections? Histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis. But these are more common in specific regions. Not sure. 

The presence of follicular hyperplasia in the lymph node biopsy might suggest a chronic antigenic stimulation, like in autoimmune disease or chronic infection. But with negative AFB, it's unclear. 

Another thought: the combination of fever, weight loss, pleural effusion, and necrotizing lymphadenitis might point towards a diagnosis like adult-onset Still's disease. But Still's usually presents with high spiking fevers, rash, and arthralgias, which aren't mentioned here. 

Alternatively, considering the patient's age and presentation, could this be a rheumatologic condition like granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA)? GPA can cause lung nodules, consolidation, and sometimes pleural effusions, as well as systemic symptoms. But typically, there's upper respiratory involvement, like sinusitis, which isn't noted here. 

The biopsy shows paracortical necrotizing lesions. Paracortical hyperplasia is often seen in viral infections, but with necrosis, it's more concerning. 

Wait, the lymph node biopsy mentions "focal paracortical necrotizing lesions with karyorrhectic debris". This might be seen in conditions like Kikuchi's disease, SLE, or even lymphoma. But since the FNAC is reactive, maybe Kikuchi's. However, Kikuchi's is typically self-limiting and not associated with pleural effusion. 

Alternatively, could this be a drug reaction? The patient hasn't mentioned any medications, but if he's on a new drug, it could cause lymphadenopathy and pleural effusion. But the history doesn't note this. 

Putting all together, the leading possibilities are malignancy (like lymphoma or metastatic cancer) versus an autoimmune/rheumatologic condition. The weight loss, fever, and clubbing lean towards malignancy. However, the reactive lymph nodes complicate that. 

In lymphoma, especially non-Hodgkin's, there can be necrotic lymph nodes. But the biopsy here shows follicular hyperplasia, which is reactive. Maybe the biopsy missed the malignant cells. Alternatively, a T-cell lymphoma could have necrosis. 

Another possibility is metastatic adenocarcinoma. For example, lung cancer could metastasize to lymph nodes, but the biopsy would show malignant cells. The fact that the FNAC and biopsy are showing reactive changes suggests that maybe it's not metastatic cancer. 

So maybe the primary is not in the lymph nodes but elsewhere. For example, mesothelioma would involve the pleura, but the lymph nodes might be reactive. However, again, exposure history is missing. 

Alternatively, could this be a paraneoplastic syndrome secondary to an occult malignancy? The patient's symptoms (weight loss, clubbing) and pleural effusion could be due to a hidden cancer elsewhere, like lung or gastrointestinal. But without more data, hard to confirm. 

Another angle: the HRCT findings of GGOs and consolidation. Could this be interstitial lung disease with associated pleural effusion? For example, NSIP or UIP with pleural involvement. But pleural effusions are not typical in IPF. However, some connective tissue diseases can cause both ILD and pleural effusions. 

But the patient has no joint symptoms or other signs of CTD. 

Let me circle back to the pleural effusion. Exudative, low ADA. Causes include malignancy, pulmonary embolism, autoimmune. Duplex Doppler ruled out DVT, so PE is less likely. Malignancy and autoimmune are left. 

The presence of clubbing is a strong indicator for chronic diseases like lung cancer, bronchiectasis, or cystic fibrosis. Given the patient's age and presentation, lung cancer is a concern. 

If the patient has lung cancer with pleural metastases, that would explain the effusion. However, the HRCT doesn't mention a mass, but atelectasis and GGOs. Sometimes, lung cancers can present as consolidations (e.g., adenocarcinoma with lepidic growth). 

Alternatively, lymphangitic carcinomatosis can present with GGOs and interlobular septal thickening. 

But again, the lymph node biopsies are not showing malignancy. 

Wait, the pleural fluid cytology—has that been done? The case summary doesn't mention it. If the pleural fluid is exudative, cytology could help detect malignant cells. But maybe it wasn't done or was negative. 

So, putting all this together, the most probable diagnosis is a malignant process, possibly lymphoma or lung cancer, despite the reactive lymph node biopsies. Alternatively, an autoimmune condition like SLE or IgG4-related disease. However, given the weight loss, clubbing, and recurrent effusions, malignancy is higher on the list. 

But the biopsy results complicate things. Let's look again: the lymph node biopsy shows follicular hyperplasia (which is reactive) and focal paracortical necrotizing lesions with karyorrhectic debris. The FNAC shows reactive lymphoid hyperplasia with necrotic changes. 

Necrotizing granulomas would suggest TB or fungal, but these are not seen. Instead, it's necrotizing lesions without granulomas. So maybe Kikuchi's, but as mentioned earlier, it's less likely in this demographic. 

Another thought: could this be a systemic vasculitis? For example, polyarteritis nodosa or another vasculitis affecting small vessels. But usually, there would be other organ involvement. 

Alternatively, considering the necrotizing lymphadenitis and pleural effusion, could this be a rare infection like tularemia or cat-scratch disease? But the history doesn't support exposure. 

Wait, the patient has no comorbid conditions. So maybe an indolent lymphoma. Some lymphomas can have a mix of reactive and malignant cells. For example, T-cell/histiocyte-rich large B-cell lymphoma can have a background of reactive cells. However, the biopsy would need to show some malignant cells. 

Alternatively, maybe the initial biopsies are not sufficient. Perhaps a repeat biopsy or a more extensive biopsy (excisional) is needed. But in the context of this case, we have to work with the given data. 

If we assume that the lymph node findings are reactive, then what systemic condition could cause reactive lymphadenopathy, necrotizing lesions, pleural effusion, weight loss, and fever? 

Autoimmune conditions like rheumatoid arthritis can cause pleural effusions, but usually with joint symptoms. Sarcoidosis can cause lymphadenopathy, but typically non-necrotizing granulomas. 

Another possibility is Castleman disease, which can present with lymphadenopathy, systemic symptoms, and pleural effusions. Castleman's is a lymphoproliferative disorder. The unicentric type can present with localized lymphadenopathy, while multicentric is systemic. However, the biopsy here shows follicular hyperplasia and necrosis, which might not align with Castleman's typical hyaline vascular or plasma cell types. 

Wait, the pleural fluid is exudative. In exudative effusions, the serum-effusion albumin gradient is less than 1.2. The low ADA makes TB unlikely. 

Another test that might help is the pleural fluid cytology. If it's negative, malignancy is less likely, but not excluded. 

The patient's clubbing is a significant sign. Clubbing is associated with lung cancer, chronic pulmonary infections, and other conditions. Given the negative infection workup, lung cancer is a concern. 

But how to tie this with the lymph node findings? If the primary is lung cancer with paraneoplastic clubbing, and reactive lymphadenopathy due to the cancer's presence, that's possible. But the HRCT doesn't show a mass. However, some cancers can present as consolidations or GGOs. 

Alternatively, maybe the patient has a lymphoma with both pulmonary involvement and pleural effusion. Lymphomas can cause mediastinal lymphadenopathy, but the biopsy here is cervical. 

Wait, the USG-guided cervical lymph node FNAC was done. If the cervical nodes are reactive, but the mediastinal nodes (not biopsied) could be involved. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy can cause pleural effusions. 

Another consideration: the patient has recurrent pleural effusions. If they are transudative, heart failure or liver disease could be considered, but the effusion is exudative. 

Given all the above, the most likely diagnosis is malignancy, specifically lymphoma or lung cancer. The reactive lymph nodes could be due to the body's response to the malignancy elsewhere. However, since the biopsies are not showing malignancy, this is a bit conflicting. 

Alternatively, a diagnosis of IgG4-related disease could explain lymphadenopathy and pleural effusion, but usually, there are elevated IgG4 levels and other organ involvement. 

Another angle: the combination of necrotizing lymphadenitis and pleural effusion with exudative features. If infections are ruled out, maybe a diagnosis like Erdheim-Chester disease, a rare non-Langerhans cell histiocytosis, but this is a stretch. 

Given the time constraints and the data provided, I think the most plausible diagnosis is lymphoma, given the systemic symptoms, weight loss, and lymph node findings, even though the biopsy shows reactive changes. Sometimes, in early stages or certain subtypes, the biopsy might not be definitive. Alternatively, the necrotizing lymphadenitis could be a result of the lymphoma. 

Another possibility is metastatic adenocarcinoma, but again, the biopsy doesn't support this. 

Alternatively, could this be a tuberculosis despite negative tests? The low ADA makes it less likely. 

After considering all possibilities, I think the leading diagnosis is lymphoma, possibly non-Hodgkin's type, with necrotizing lymphadenitis and paraneoplastic manifestations causing pleural effusion, fever, and weight loss. The reactive findings on biopsy could be due to sampling error or a surrounding reactive process. 

Alternatively, given the necrotizing lesions in lymph nodes, Kikuchi's disease, but given the patient's age and the presence of pleural effusion, this is less likely. 

Another rare possibility is Rosai-Dorfman disease, but it typically presents with massive lymphadenopathy and emperipolesis on biopsy. 

In conclusion, after weighing the possibilities, the most likely diagnosis is lymphoma, even though the biopsy isn't confirmatory. Alternatively, if not lymphoma, then a paraneoplastic syndrome from an occult malignancy, possibly lung cancer. However, given the information provided, the answer is likely lymphoma.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with recurrent bilateral exudative pleural effusions, systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss), and necrotizing lymphadenopathy. Infectious causes like tuberculosis are less likely due to negative AFB/Trunet assays and low pleural fluid ADA.  
<step 2> Malignancy is strongly suspected due to age, weight loss, clubbing, and exudative effusions. However, lymph node biopsies show reactive follicular hyperplasia and necrotizing lesions without overt malignancy, complicating the diagnosis.  
<step 3> The necrotizing lymphadenitis raises differentials like Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease, SLE, or lymphoma. Kikuchi’s is unlikely in this demographic, and SLE lacks supporting clinical features.  
<step 4> HRCT findings (GGOs, consolidation) and clubbing suggest pulmonary involvement, possibly from malignancy (e.g., lung cancer or lymphoma) or interstitial lung disease. Lack of masses on imaging reduces lung cancer likelihood but doesn’t exclude it.  
<step 5> Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia on biopsy could indicate paraneoplastic lymphadenopathy secondary to occult malignancy. Lymphoma remains plausible despite inconclusive biopsies, as sampling errors or early-stage disease may mask malignant cells.  

### Answer:
**Lymphoma**

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with recurrent bilateral exudative pleural effusions, systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss), and necrotizing lymphadenopathy. Infectious causes like tuberculosis are less likely due to negative AFB/Trunet assays and low pleural fluid ADA.

Malignancy is strongly suspected due to age, weight loss, clubbing, and exudative effusions. However, lymph node biopsies show reactive follicular hyperplasia and necrotizing lesions without overt malignancy, complicating the diagnosis.
The necrotizing lymphadenitis raises differentials like Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease, SLE, or lymphoma. Kikuchi’s is unlikely in this demographic, and SLE lacks supporting clinical features.

HRCT findings (GGOs, consolidation) and clubbing suggest pulmonary involvement, possibly from malignancy (e.g., lung cancer or lymphoma) or interstitial lung disease. Lack of masses on imaging reduces lung cancer likelihood but doesn’t exclude it.

Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia on biopsy could indicate paraneoplastic lymphadenopathy secondary to occult malignancy. Lymphoma remains plausible despite inconclusive biopsies, as sampling errors or early-stage disease may mask malignant cells.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: The patient's signs and symptoms (prolonged fever, dyspnea, pleural effusion, lymphadenopathy, and weight loss) prompted consideration of infectious, neoplastic, or autoimmune conditions.

Step 2: Tuberculosis was initially considered but ruled out based on negative acid-fast bacillus tests, Trunet assay, and exudative pleural effusion with low ADA levels; bacterial infections were less likely based on the absence of pleural fluid culture growth.
Step 3: Lymphoma was considered due to significant lymphadenopathy, but cervical lymph node FNAC reported reactive hyperplasia, excluding malignancy.
Step 4: Kikuchi-Fujimoto Disease (KFD) was suspected due to the histopathological findings (necrotic lesions with karyorrhectic debris, reactive lymphoid hyperplasia).
Step 5: Systemic lupus erythematosus was ruled out based on the lack of clinical or serological evidence.
Step 6: FNAC findings supported KFD diagnosis, confirming the presence of reactive lymphoid hyperplasia with necrotic changes characteristic of KFD.
Step 7: Confirmatory tests (negative infectious markers and biopsy findings) helped exclude other causes.
Step 8: The final diagnosis was Kikuchi-Fujimoto Disease (KFD) based on clinical presentation and confirmatory FNAC findings.
Step 9: The absence of evidence for infectious, neoplastic, or autoimmune conditions further supported the conclusion.
Step 10: The patient responded favorably to corticosteroid therapy, consistent with the management of KFD.
